title: Global wood harvest is sufficient for climate-friendly transitions to timber cities
data_sources: World Bank waste database (Kaza et al.)

data_scope.spatial_coverage: Global, disaggregated into 12 MAgPIE world regions (CAZ, CHA, EUR, IND, JPN, LAM, MEA, NEU, OAS, REF, SSA, USA)
data_scope.scenario_urban_coverage: 90% of new urban population in timber buildings
data_scope.plantation_forest_expansion_harvest: 168 Mha (128% expansion vs 2021 baseline of 131 Mha)

core_conclusion: Transitioning 90% of the global new urban population to engineered timber buildings does not require more wood harvest: the shift pathway (redirecting 40% of wood fuel to industrial use) or circular pathway (raising wood-waste recycling from 30% to 57%) can meet timber city demand within current harvest volume, reducing cumulative CO2-eq. emissions by 40.8 Gt by 2100 versus business as usual. The most critical global actions are reducing virgin wood combustion for bioenergy and scaling wood-waste recycling, not expanding plantation forests.
why_it_matters: First probabilistic global wood-cycle quantification across 12 world regions using BaMFA; resolves the key supply-side question for timber cities; reveals bioenergy as the dominant bottleneck (67% of wood end use, ~10x the construction emission savings); exposes dangerous regional disparities for developing regions; delivers open data and interactive Sankey diagrams.
why_nature: Global systems perspective integrating land use, energy, material flows, and GHG across 12 regions; timely policy relevance as timber-city proposals enter national net-zero strategies; novel BaMFA methodology with explicit uncertainty propagation; cross-sectoral interdisciplinary scope; full open-science data and code release.
added_value_to_my_research: Spatially explicit timber-demand mapping (WorldPop + GHS + FAOSTAT) -> wood-supply accessibility surface analogous to R2SFCA service-area delineation
SHAP-interpreted GBM on BaMFA recycling-rate heterogeneity -> identifies policy levers driving circular-rate gaps across regions, extending explainable-ML pipeline to environmental material flows
Coupling BaMFA scenarios with building-energy models -> integrated embodied+operational carbon budget for timber cities, informing urban health policy via air-quality and heat-island co-benefits
Monte Carlo ensemble over renewable-energy substitution uncertainty -> physics-informed uncertainty propagation extended from epidemiological SIR to environmental material-flow systems
Network community detection on global timber surplus-deficit flows -> optimal inter-regional trade corridors, directly transferring job-housing network methods to commodity supply chains

related.title: Global spatial assessment of potential for new peri-urban forests to combat climate change
related.relation: global spatial analysis of urban forest carbon potential — methodological complement to timber-supply spatial disaggregation

related.title: The climate limits of construction in over 1,000 cities
related.relation: city-level construction GHG limits — directly relevant to evaluating where timber cities would have the highest climate benefit

related.title: Exploiting vacant urban residential buildings to promote carbon neutrality in China
related.relation: urban building-stock circular strategies reducing construction demand — parallel circular-economy approach to embodied carbon
